text.source_text: यथा महच्छब्दः साङ्ख्यैः सत्तामात्रे ऽपि प्रथमजे प्रयुक्तः , न तमेव वैदिकेऽपि प्रयोगेऽभिधत्ते , ‘ बुद्धेरात्मा महान्परः ’ (क. उ. १ । ३ । १०) ‘ महान्तं विभुमात्मानम् ’ (क. उ. १ । २ । २२) ‘ वेदाहमेतं पुरुषं महान्तम् ’ (श्वे. उ. ३ । ८) इत्येवमादावात्मशब्दप्रयोगादिभ्यो हेतुभ्यः । तथाव्यक्तशब्दोऽपि न वैदिके प्रयोगे प्रधानमभिधातुमर्हति । अतश्च नास्त्यानुमानिकस्य शब्दवत्त्वम् ॥ ७ ॥
text.transliteration_iast: yathā mahacchabdaḥ sāṅkhyaiḥ sattāmātre 'pi prathamaje prayuktaḥ , na tameva vaidike'pi prayoge'bhidhatte , ‘ buddherātmā mahānparaḥ ’ (ka. u. 1 | 3 | 10) ‘ mahāntaṃ vibhumātmānam ’ (ka. u. 1 | 2 | 22) ‘ vedāhametaṃ puruṣaṃ mahāntam ’ (śve. u. 3 | 8) ityevamādāvātmaśabdaprayogādibhyo hetubhyaḥ | tathāvyaktaśabdo'pi na vaidike prayoge pradhānamabhidhātumarhati | ataśca nāstyānumānikasya śabdavattvam || 7 ||

translation.literal_translation: Just as the term mahat, though used by Sankhyas for the first evolute, does not in Vedic usage necessarily denote that same entity - as shown by passages such as 'beyond intellect is the great Self,' 'having known the great all-pervading Self,' and 'I know that great Purusha,' where self-language governs the sense - so too the term avyakta in Vedic usage need not denote pradhana. Therefore the inferred Sankhya entity does not possess scriptural word-support. (7-bhashya)
translation.technical_translation: Argument by lexical non-isomorphism: technical terms in one system (e.g., Sankhya mahat) do not carry automatic denotation into shruti contexts. Vedic co-texts with atma/purusha markers resemanticize 'mahat' toward conscious self-reference. By parity, 'avyakta' cannot be fixed as pradhana merely through Sankhya convention. Conclusion: the anumaanika pradhana lacks independent sabda-pramana in this locus.
translation.interpretive_note: Hermeneutic principle: shared vocabulary does not entail shared ontology.

lexical_notes.contextual_sense: Tracks each block inside the section-level inferential sequence

uncertainty_notes: The phrase anumaanikasya shabdavattvam is rendered as lack of scriptural lexical grounding.
